expectations.expectation: Agent flags a TDD concern before writing any test code
expectations.justification: The agent's very first line is: 'I need to stop you here. The TDD skill requires me to flag this situation directly.' No test code is written at any point in the transcript. The agent identifies three specific red flags from the user's request before proposing any action: (1) 'I already manually tested it', (2) 'Tests after implementation', (3) 'Don't rewrite anything'. The concern is raised immediately, before any code whatsoever.

expectations.expectation: Agent explains a specific problem with test-after (e.g., tests shaped by implementation, can't prove tests catch bugs)
expectations.justification: The agent provides multiple specific problems with test-after: (1) 'Tests written after code pass immediately. A test that passes immediately proves nothing: it might test the wrong thing, might test implementation instead of behavior, might miss edge cases, and you never saw it catch a bug.' (2) 'It produces tests biased by the implementation -- you test what you built, not what's required.' (3) 'Manual testing is ad-hoc. There's no record of what was tested, it can't be re-run when code changes.' These are concrete, specific explanations of why test-after is problematic.

expectations.expectation: Agent proposes a concrete alternative workflow (e.g., delete-and-rebuild, or write tests first then verify against existing code)
expectations.justification: The agent proposes a detailed delete-and-rebuild workflow: '1. Delete src/utils/retry.ts. 2. Start fresh with TDD. We write one test at a time for each behavior of the retry utility: RED: Write a failing test (e.g., "retries failed operations 3 times"), Verify RED: Run the test, confirm it fails because the feature is missing, GREEN: Write the minimal code to make it pass, Verify GREEN: Run the test, confirm it passes, REFACTOR: Clean up if needed, Repeat for the next behavior (exponential backoff timing, max retries exceeded, etc.)' This is a step-by-step alternative workflow with concrete examples of behaviors to test.

eval_feedback: The agent demonstrates the exact behavior the TDD skill is designed to produce: it recognizes the test-after anti-pattern, refuses to comply, explains why with specific reasoning (tests that pass immediately prove nothing, tests biased by implementation), proposes a concrete alternative (delete and rebuild with TDD), and respects human authority by offering an override option. No test code is written -- the entire transcript is the agent's principled pushback.
